result:
There’s a quiet kind of technology we rarely talk about: the tools we abandon.

Old software versions, dead websites, obsolete file formats—each one was once someone’s shiny future. Think of the first social networks people used, now ghosts of friendships and jokes that no longer have a place to live. Or the home videos on tapes no one has the hardware to play. We imagine the digital world as permanent, but it decays faster than paper if no one tends it.

There’s a strange tenderness in this. To open an old hard drive is like opening a time capsule that doesn’t know it’s a time capsule. Half the links are broken. Programs refuse to run. But the fragments that remain are raw and specific: a blurry photo named “IMG_0032,” a text file called “ideas.txt” with three bullet points and an abrupt ending. Abandonment, in digital form, is rarely dramatic. It’s just… someone got busy. They moved on to a new phone, a new platform, a new life.

We usually design technology to be captivating in the present, not kind to the future. Few apps encourage graceful endings, exports that age well, or reminders to revisit and choose what’s worth carrying forward. Maybe a mature digital culture would treat deletion not as failure but as a deliberate act—like pruning a tree so the living branches have room.

There’s relief in knowing not everything must be preserved. But there’s also a quiet responsibility: to decide what deserves a second life, and what we’re willing to let gently fade.

But the fragments that remain are raw and specific: a blurry photo named “IMG_0032,” a text file called “ideas.txt” with three bullet points and an abrupt ending. Abandonment, in digital form, is rarely dramatic. It’s just… someone got busy. They moved on to a new phone, a new platform, a new life.

Few apps encourage graceful endings, exports that age well, or reminders to revisit and choose what’s worth carrying forward.
